**Carl Hoefkens** (born 6 October 1978) is a Belgian football manager and former professional [footballer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") who played as a [defender](/wiki/Defender_(association_football) "Defender (association football)"). He is currently the manager of [Eredivisie](/wiki/Eredivisie "Eredivisie") club [NAC Breda](/wiki/NAC_Breda "NAC Breda").

## Club career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Hoefkens&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Club career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Hoefkens&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early career")]

Born in [Lier](/wiki/Lier,_Belgium "Lier, Belgium"), Hoefkens started his career at [Lierse](/wiki/Lierse_S.K._(1906) "Lierse S.K. (1906)") and played there for six years. He made his breakthrough with Lierse in first division and won the [Belgian Cup](/wiki/Belgian_Cup "Belgian Cup") in 1999, beating [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège") in the final with 3–1. Afterwards, he made a move to [Lommel](/wiki/K.F.C._Lommel_S.K. "K.F.C. Lommel S.K.") but Lommel went bankrupt in 2003. In the summer of 2003, he came to [Germinal Beerschot](/wiki/K.F.C._Germinal_Beerschot "K.F.C. Germinal Beerschot"), a first division team in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"). There, he became one of the pillars of the team. In his second season at Germinal Beerschot, he won the cup in the final against [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_K.V. "Club Brugge K.V."), then champions of Belgium. Hoefkens is still appreciated by Beerschot fans; a group of fans from the club have travelled to England on numerous occasions to watch him play.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Stoke City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Hoefkens&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Stoke City")]

In July 2005, [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") manager [Johan Boskamp](/wiki/Johan_Boskamp "Johan Boskamp") signed Hoefkens on a two-year contract, for an undisclosed fee.[[3]](#cite_note-3) He made his debut for the club in a 0–0 draw against [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C.") on 6 August 2005. Hoefkens became an ever-present in the Stoke line-up and wore the number 2 shirt. He was named 'Fans' Player of the year ([2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football "2005–06 in English football")).

During the January 2007 [transfer window](/wiki/Transfer_window "Transfer window"), he was linked with a return to Belgium, with [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_K.V. "Club Brugge K.V.") reportedly interested in him. He was also [penalty](/wiki/Penalty_kick_(association_football) "Penalty kick (association football)") taker for Stoke before the arrival of [Danny Higginbotham](/wiki/Danny_Higginbotham "Danny Higginbotham"); however, he still maintained a 100% record for the club. In the [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_in_English_football "2006–07 in English football") season, he operated as a [winger](/wiki/Midfielder#Winger "Midfielder") or a [central midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder#Central_midfielder "Midfielder") on occasions in the latter stages of the season.

### West Bromwich Albion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Hoefkens&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: West Bromwich Albion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Carl_Hoefkens_20071020.jpg/250px-Carl_Hoefkens_20071020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carl_Hoefkens_20071020.jpg)

Hoefkens with [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") in 2007

Stoke accepted a bid for Hoefkens from [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") on 4 August 2007.[[4]](#cite_note-West_Brom_close_on_Hoefkens_deal-4) He joined Albion on 7 August 2007 in a £750,000 deal and was offered a two-year contract plus a further one-year as an option.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Hoefkens made his Albion debut in a 2–1 defeat away at Burnley on the opening day of the [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_in_English_football "2007–08 in English football") season.[[6]](#cite_note-6) One week later, Hoefkens was named in the Championship Team of the Week, following his performance in the 2–0 home win over [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.").[[7]](#cite_note-7) He is known by his West Brom teammates as "[Wolverine](/wiki/Wolverine_(character) "Wolverine (character)")", due to his resemblance to the comic book hero from [X-Men](/wiki/X-Men "X-Men").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Hoefkens was released in the summer of 2009.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Return to Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Hoefkens&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Return to Belgium")]

On 25 August 2009, [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge "Club Brugge") signed the former [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion "West Bromwich Albion")'s Belgian right-back on a two-year deal. He became captain in the season 2010–11. After the season ended, he signed for an extra year with the club.[[10]](#cite_note-10) After four years and 127 appearances for the club, Hoefkens moved to [Lierse](/wiki/Lierse_S.K._(1906) "Lierse S.K. (1906)") in 2013, spending a season there before a move to [Oostende](/wiki/K.V._Oostende "K.V. Oostende").

### Gibraltar and retirement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Hoefkens&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Gibraltar and retirement")]

In August 2015, after his release from Oostende, Hoefkens signed for [Gibraltar Premier Division](/wiki/Gibraltar_Premier_Division "Gibraltar Premier Division") side [Manchester 62](/wiki/Manchester_62_F.C. "Manchester 62 F.C."), who beat off competition from reigning champions [Lincoln Red Imps](/wiki/Lincoln_Red_Imps_F.C. "Lincoln Red Imps F.C.") for his signature. He signed undisclosed semi-professional terms for the side and aided in the development of David Ochello's young side, making his debut on 26 September in a 1–0 victory over [Glacis United](/wiki/Glacis_United_F.C. "Glacis United F.C."). After 19 league games and two goals, as the Red Devils of Gibraltar finished sixth in the league, he retired at the end of the [2015–16 season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Gibraltar_Premier_Division "2015–16 Gibraltar Premier Division").

## International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Hoefkens&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: International career")]

Hoefkens played 22 times for the [Belgium national team](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team"), scoring once. He also represented Belgium in the [1997 FIFA World Youth Championship](/wiki/1997_FIFA_World_Youth_Championship "1997 FIFA World Youth Championship").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Hoefkens' one international goal came on 24 May 2006, in a friendly with [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") in [Genk](/wiki/Genk "Genk"). His last-minute goal secured a 3–3 draw.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It was feared that Hoefkens had broken a bone in his foot in a [Euro 2008](/wiki/Euro_2008 "Euro 2008") qualifier against [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") in March 2007, however a scan revealed that there was no damage.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Coaching and later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Hoefkens&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Coaching and later career")]

At the end of January 2018, Hoefkens was hired as a scout for [Knokke](/wiki/Royal_Knokke_F.C. "Royal Knokke F.C.").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

On 28 May 2018, it was confirmed that Hoefkens would return to [Club Brugge](/wiki/Club_Brugge_K.V. "Club Brugge K.V.") from the upcoming season, where he would function as assistant coach and talent coach for the U21 and U18 teams.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In June 2019, Hoefkens was placed in a new role as a part of the first team staff, where he would have the specific task of guiding young talents and provide the link between the academy and the first team.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) In May 2022, Hoefkens took over as head coach of Club Brugge, signing a contract with indefinite duration.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In the [2022–23 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_UEFA_Champions_League "2022–23 UEFA Champions League"), he led Club Brugge to the [knockout phase](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_UEFA_Champions_League_knockout_phase "2022–23 UEFA Champions League knockout phase") for the first time in the Champions League era.[[19]](#cite_note-19) On 28 December 2022, Club Brugge announced the termination of his contract.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Hoefkens was named as manager of [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège") on 16 June 2023, on a three-year deal. He replaced [Ronny Deila](/wiki/Ronny_Deila "Ronny Deila"), who went to Brugge.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Hoefkens named [Yaya Touré](/wiki/Yaya_Tour%C3%A9 "Yaya Touré") as part of his coaching staff.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Later that year, on 31 December, he was dismissed by the club due to disappointing results.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Hoefkens was then announced as [NAC Breda](/wiki/NAC_Breda "NAC Breda") manager on the 6 June 2024 for the 2024–25 season in the [Eredivisie](/wiki/Eredivisie "Eredivisie"), which was NAC's first season in the division since 2018–19.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carl_Hoefkens&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Hoefkens married model Vanessa D'Hooghe in 2001.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
